fact: May 18 is exactly 138 days into the year, which means there are exactly 227 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for baby rabbits to be hopping around everywhere - they're born with their eyes closed but open them around this time of year!
The sun usually rises before 6 AM in most of North America on May 18, making it one of those days when early birds get treated to spectacular orange and pink skies.
Dandelions are often going crazy right now, shooting their fluffy white seed parachutes into the air - one dandelion can produce up to 2,000 seeds!
May 18 falls in Taurus season, which runs from approximately April 20 to May 20 in the zodiac calendar.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, May 18 is deep into autumn, and leaves are typically turning brilliant reds and golds while everyone up north is enjoying spring flowers.
This date sits exactly one month before the summer solstice in the north, meaning daylight is stretching longer every single day by about 2 minutes!
Mosquitoes are often just starting to become super annoying around this time in many temperate regions because the weather is finally warm enough for them to breed.
May 18 is typically prime migration time for millions of songbirds flying north, so if you look up, you might spot flocks of warblers, thrushes, and other feathered travelers.
When there's a full moon on May 18, it's called the 'Flower Moon' because of all the blooming happening everywhere during this time of year.
In many northern regions, this is usually when maple trees finish growing their full summer leaves after weeks of tiny green buds.
May 18 often marks the time when many temperate places hit that perfect 'Goldilocks weather' - not too hot, not too cold, but just right for spending all day outside without a jacket.
